perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of California and the laws of Hong Kong.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally interpreted under the principles of law common to both the State of California and Hong Kong.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law clause to a general interpretation of principles introduces ambiguity. It's unclear which specific laws apply and how conflicts between California and Hong Kong law would be resolved. This ambiguity makes enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1636 states that 'A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful.' The modification replaces the clear directive to enforce 'in accordance with' specific laws, undermining the ascertainable intention of the parties and creating uncertainty that conflicts with the precise interpretation mandated by California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding which laws govern the agreement, potentially conflicting with California Civil Code Section 1636. The original text explicitly states adherence to the laws of California and Hong Kong, whereas the changed text refers to general principles common to both. The law snippet states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, 'so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful'. By moving away from specific laws to general principles, the ascertainable intention becomes less clear and more open to interpretation, thus creating a contradiction. The modification weakens the binding nature of specific legal requirements, making enforcement uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: The Sponsor shall retain all of its rights under copyright and trademark laws pertaining to any of its intellectual property.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sponsor shall endeavor to retain its rights relating to its intellectual property.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall retain' to 'shall endeavor to retain' weakens the Sponsor's protection of its intellectual property, making the enforcement of those rights uncertain. The original phrasing provides a definitive legal protection under copyright and trademark law; the new phrasing creates a more discretionary interpretation. 'Endeavor' does not ensure protection under law.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 106 grants copyright owners exclusive rights to their work. By stating the sponsor will merely 'endeavor' to retain rights, the contract creates ambiguity about the extent to which the sponsor is actually protected, undermining the statutory rights granted by federal copyright law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 106 - Exclusive rights in copyrighted works | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 106 - Exclusive rights in copyrighted works U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords ; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental, lease, or lending; (4) in the case of literary, musical, dramatic, and choreographic works, pantomimes, and motion pictures and other audiovisual works , to perform the copyrighted work publicly; (5) in the case of literary, musical, dramatic, and choreographic works, pantomimes, and pictorial, graphic, or sculptural works, including the individual images of a motion picture or other audiovisual work, to display the copyrighted work publicly; and (6) in the case of sound recordings , to perform the copyrighted work publicly by means of a digital audio transmission. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2546 ; Pub. L. 101–318, § 3(d) , July 3, 1990 , 104 Stat. 288 ; Pub. L. 101–650, title VII, § 704(b)(2) , Dec. 1, 1990 , 104 Stat. 5134 ; Pub. L. 104–39, § 2 , Nov. 1, 1995 , 109 Stat. 336 ; Pub. L. 106–44, § 1(g)(2) , Aug. 5, 1999 , 113 Stat. 222 ; Pub. L. 107–273, div. C, title III, § 13210(4)(A) , Nov. 2, 2002 , 116 Stat. 1909 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 General Scope of Copyright. The five fundamental rights that the bill gives to copyright owners—the exclusive rights of reproduction, adaptation, publication, performance, and display— are stated generally in section 106. Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental,
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the Sponsor's obligation to protect its intellectual property rights. The original text, 'shall retain,' indicates a mandatory action, aligning with 17 U.S.C. § 106, which grants copyright owners 'exclusive rights' to their work. By changing it to 'shall endeavor to retain,' the contract introduces ambiguity, suggesting the Sponsor may not fully enforce its rights. This undermines the 'exclusive rights' guaranteed by copyright law. The change introduces Ambiguity into the contract with the new wording, making it less enforceable, which conflicts with the protections that are granted by law.
